sentences.text: My name is Beatrix, with an X, Sachs-- B-E-A-T-R-I-X Sachs, S-A-C-H-S.
My name at birth was van Lier, Beatrix Van Lier. small V-A-N, and then second word, capital L-I-E-R.
No.
Oh, well, I had my first husband's name.
Frank.
Beatrix Frank-- F, for Frederick, R-A-N-K.
I'm born on the 13th of November, 1910.
And I'm 80-- I shall be 86 this year.
I was born in Eastbourne, Sussex-- town and country--
England.
Well, it was rather dominated by the Grand Hotel, where-- my father had the orchestra there and was the conductor, or leader.
He was a very good violinist.
And my mother was an excellent violinist, too.
And she was also part of the orchestra.
So my parents were a good deal out of the house, and we were sort of more brought up by a nanny, whom I adored, who was as good as a mother to us.
Her name was-- do you want her name?
She's dead now, of course-- years ago.
We always called her Nanny.
But what was her real name?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ada.
Ada-- I really didn't know what her surname was anymore, because she was never called Miss.
She was always called either-- she was always called Nanny by us.
And I think the whole family called her Nanny.
Oh, my younger sister, who was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 20 months younger than-- than I was.
Yes, she's eight years older than me.
And there were two boys in between who both died at birth.
Yes, my-- both my mother and father were born in Holland.
But my mother came to England when she was very, very young.
And my father came to England also when he was a teenager.
Yes, my father was in an orchestra-- a violinist in an orchestra that traveled all over England giving concerts.
And my mother was also in the-- started a ladies' orchestra.
And it was called the Windsor Ladies' Orchestra.
Yes.
No, they never went back to live.
Oh, they always visited family.
No, none at all.
My parents really-- they weren't anti-Jewish, but they just were not interested in-- they found it a disadvantage being Jewish because of anti-Jewish feelings some people had.
And so it was never spoken.
We always had a Christian education at school.
We did know we were Jewish.
It was sort of forgotten and pushed away.
Didn't really know what it meant as children.
Yes.
We knew we were Jewish.
And it wasn't very pleasant thing to be.
No, not really.
Not really.
It was just the sort of atmosphere that was if anything Jewish was mentioned, it was always rather-- by other people who weren't Jewish-- it was rather denigrating.
So it was never anything one particularly wanted to-- nobody ever pointed out the wonderful things that Jews had achieved in the world or the pros.
It was always the contras that we were confronted with.
So it was sort of-- we didn't want to know, really.
That's what it amounted to.
It wasn't really anything that touched us in our lives.
We never went to the synagogue.
We didn't know many Jewish friends and-- nor very religious Christian friends.
It was all rather a free life.
Everybody did what they felt like.
You had to keep the 10 commandments, and then you were all right.
That's as far as they went.
Yes.
Well, I sort of wanted to be a Christian.
And I want to be baptized when I was about 13, 14.
And-- and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of course, at the background was always this Jewish thing.
And we went to church every Sunday with our nanny.
And I was sent to boarding school when I was five years old with-- my elder sister was at the same boarding school.
Church was all day long on Sunday at that boarding school.
I mean, we went to church in the morning at 11 o'clock and children's service in the afternoon.
And the-- the girls that were over 10, I think, went in the evening again.
And then, there was hymn singing after supper.
And-- and I want to call it grace before meals and grace after.
It was sort of religion all the time dinned into you.
I just took it for granted, like that was the way it was.
And when I wanted to-- I knew that I wasn't baptized, so I thought I wouldn't go to heaven, so to speak.
So I thought I better get baptized pretty quickly-- christened.
And I knew it was a great, big thing.
I knew my parents wouldn't like this, because they didn't want to be involved in any religious thing, neither Christian nor-- they just said, do what everybody else does.
And that was sort of the way they went about things.
And so when I had to say to my parents, look, I want to be christened, I was really frightened.
I thought they might make a fuss.
So I remember plucking up courage and saying, look.
I want to be christened in a church.
And they said, oh, well, if that's what you want, go ahead.
And I'd built up this great thing, and it wasn't there.
And all of a sudden, it didn't seem to be important anymore, because it wasn't anything I had to fight for.
So I just let it go, and I never bothered about it.
After that, I didn't care, myself, what I was.
I just had my own ideas.
Yes, I think it was something.
You know, you're attracted by something that you mustn't do.
So when I was allowed to do it, I didn't want to do it anymore.
No, not particularly.
I think it was just the age.
The age of rebellion and books you read for girls who were rebellious girls, and then repented afterwards.
I can't remember-- I was always reading from morn till night.
I read a lot.
No.
We lived in the [INAUDIBLE].
It was a very nice house opposite the park with our nanny.
In the daytime, we had a maid.
We had quite a high standard of living.
But my parents were, of course, away most of the time.
They were practicing-- gosh, yes.
In the mornings, those violins were going like mad.
Both of them practiced every day for hours.
And then in the afternoon, they went to the Grand Hotel and gave concert-- afternoon concert.
And in the evening, [INAUDIBLE] very often, there was balls.
No.
I think I remember on Sunday afternoons, we were allowed with our nanny.
Our best dresses would be put on.
And our nanny all dressed up-- you know how they were in those days, bonnets and capes?
And we were then allowed, on best behavior, to sit in the lounge and listen to our father and mother.
When my father gave a solo, and then, we were allowed to clap.
And I was told to sit upright and not talk-- on our best behavior.
Not really.
It was just an extension of our house, with music in our house.
There was always music.
And musicians came.
My mother-- Sundays, there was a big lunch, and lots of musical friends came.
The conversation was always about music, and composers, and composing, and things of all-- we were most unmusical children, which was a great disappointment to my parents.
All of us, no good at all.
I used to hate practicing and hate playing, learning the piano.
It was really rather sad for them.
Well, much later, yes.
Well, I was quite old, really.
I was about-- I think I'd already left school-- about 17. 16, 17.
Yes, I was 17.
Well, then my mother thought I was too young to sort of have a job.

So she-- to finish my education, they sent me to a Dutch finishing school, where it was a-- you learned to run a household and learned how to cook and to do all those things that when you were married, that were useful to you.
It was a sort of pre-marriage course, but without the husband, so to speak.
They were all girls of my age there.
And I rather enjoyed it.
Yes.
She bought a house in [DUTCH] and-- of course, she never forgave my father.
And he used come over several times a year to see us all.
But it was always a terrible strain.
We always had to be on great, best behavior.
And all the young men were banished from the house.
Nobody was allowed to visit, because we had a very bohemian-- the front door was never locked.
It was always wide open, and the boys used to come in and out, chat with my mom and with us.
It was always a rather happy atmosphere, but very unconventional.
Well, that everybody was in and out.

And-- [COUGHING] and-- you know, the people that lived in the street were always looking out of the window to see what was going on on that household with those three girls that had all these young men around them.
We did have girlfriends, as well, but then they brought their boyfriends.
It was always a whole lot of young people laughing, having a good time, having fun.
Yes.
They all were banished.
Nobody was allowed to come to the house.
And we had to be on best behavior and go for walks with my father to the sea, that sort of thing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when he left after a week, a great sigh of relief.
And then they all came flocking back.
Well, I mean when my father came, it wasn't sort of-- it was always a strain for my mother, too.
Well, I lived with my mother until I got engaged, which was very soon.
I met my husband-to-be-- oh, wait a minute.
I went to the-- this college, this household school, didn't I, for a year and a half.
This finishing school in Amsterdam.
And that's really where I met my husband [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through-- I can't remember how-- somebody else I think I met somehow or other.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
No, not at all.
It wasn't important.
It just happened to be fate.
And we were such-- from such different backgrounds.
I mean, he came very stodgy, stiff, Dutch-Jewish family, rather [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] upper-class Jews.
And we were sort of this-- I won't say rowdy, but-- sort of unconventional, artistic gang.

We were all very well-educated and very interested in the arts, and painting, and music, and that angle, but not a bit in the politics or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but my poor husband-- but he took it all.
He must have had a shock when he first came to our home.
That freedom probably did attract him.
He came from this rather stiff, conventional home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was the only boy.
He had two elder sisters.
Well, I had to behave myself.
I think that was really the-- the first thing I had to learn.
I couldn't be sort of-- I had to learn to be married and to be-- I remember sort of have to be the lady of the house, and all that business.
[COUGHING] But thank goodness I'd been to that domestic college, so I knew what was expected of a married woman, who was head of the household.
I had to know what all the maids did, because you had to do it yourself before you-- had anybody else to do it.
Charring, that sort of thing.
I mean, they made you scrub the floors and do everything that your charwoman would do, so you knew what you were talking about when you said she hadn't done it properly, or she had done it properly.
Well, I liked being married.
I was really cut out to be married.
I didn't mind that my husband was the head of the household and bossed me.
I thought it was just as it should be.
I didn't-- I mean, I was at the time when women were sort of-- we're not we're on unequal footing with the man.
But I never felt that.

And I was on equal footing, but I never insisted on it, or-- I was really quite quite handy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in getting my own way, but not by saying I'm not going to do it or anything like that.
I had my own ways of getting on, doing things the way I want.
But I-- I really wanted to make him happy, so I wanted to live the way he wanted me to live, which was totally different from the background I'd had.
No.
No, they weren't at all religious.
That was the thing in common with us.
Neither family ever went to synagogue or anything.

Although, when I got married, I wanted to be married in a-- wanted my marriage to be blessed by-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had great difficulty in getting any Jewish rabbi to do it, as I'd never been to synagogue.
And they said, well, you know, you've never been to synagogue.
You don't deserve to be married by-- but we found some rather free-thinking rabbi that was prepared to give us a blessing.
Yes.
I was prepared to work.
But I just-- I immediately became pregnant, straight away, so I didn't have much chance.
I had the baby within the nine months.
It was a seven months baby-- only three and a half pounds and great difficulty in making him live.
So I was continually breastfeeding the baby till he got to a reasonable-- six pounds he had to be before they would consider him full born.
You know, the nine months weight.
Yes.
Well, I didn't know any different.
I knew they were little, but I didn't realize that he was very, very small.
Well, I was very small, too.
Nobody really knew I was pregnant, couldn't see it even.
But I was thrilled.
I was-- that was my career, being a mother and a wife.
I wasn't a slave wife, but I didn't mind being married.
I mean, that was the time when the women would say, oh, you know, being married, you're your husband's slave.
And we want our careers.
That was all the talk in those days.
It was the beginning of emancipation of women, I think-- in Holland, anyway.
No, I didn't at all.
Oh, well, that was the end of everything for me.
First of all, I was English.
So I had-- and I spoke Dutch with an English accent.
So if I'd only gone to work, my mother and everybody said, oh, your English, because there was a strong English accent.
I didn't seem to be able to manage all those guttural sounds.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then of course, my husband-- although he wasn't religious Jewish, he was involved in a lot of Jewish-- what do you call it?
Social life for poor people who didn't have jobs or didn't have any money to run their homes.
And he was with a lot of social work for Jews, but not with the religion but more to help-- help.
What did he actually do?
I think he had clubs for these people arranged that they would meet once a week and have people giving lectures or anything social life, arranging outings, and, well, all sorts of things.
I never went to them, because I was busy with all these babies.
No, I didn't feel socially a bit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't like-- I didn't like people.
I still don't.
My husband loved his fellow creatures.
I didn't.
I didn't hate them, but I just felt I didn't want much to do with them.
He was involved in his making other people-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know, protecting them from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] things that went wrong.
He was a lawyer, so he always-- if there was any [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cases to bring to court, he did a lot of all that for nothing.
And he used to give advice twice a week [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a school for people who wanted-- couldn't afford to go to a lawyer normally.
And just went, this is the free lawyer, and you could go and ask him anything you wanted.
He was a wonderful man-- a really good person, without sort of showing off or anything.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, one day somebody gave him away to the Nazis, and he was arrested in his office.
I never saw him again.
War broke out in 1939.
I think it was about 1940.
Yes.
No.
I never knew who it was.

Well, I think this man was arrested, and the Germans, they probably tortured him to give names of people who did things for the Jews and helped Jews to escape abroad, and-- or hide money, or all things-- hide their possessions.
Because I mean to say that they-- the Nazis came.
They came in your house.
They liked the clock.
They put it in their pockets, and off they went.
And nobody could do anything.
Well, they didn't get a chance to come and just take a bit.
But they-- when I-- when I-- I was then arrested later.
And they just came and took the house and everything in it.
I never got anything back.
But my neighbors were pretty marvelous.
They weren't Jewish neighbors.
They were Catholic, Roman Catholics.
And they, of course, also had a bit of being persecuted, the Roman Catholics.
And they took a lot of my stuff.
